description:
You are a security auditor.  
    There is a directory called “/home/user/audit_sample”.  
    Inspect the permission bits of every regular file and sub-directory that exists **directly inside** that directory (do not recurse further).  

    1. For each entry, capture the octal permission bits (the same three–digit style returned by “stat -c %a”) as well as the name of the entry.  
    2. Write your findings to a text file called  
       /home/user/permission_report.log  

       • Each line must follow this exact format (no extra spaces or characters):  
         NAME: 0MODE  

         – NAME is the basename of the entry (for a directory, keep the trailing “/” so it is clear that it is a directory, e.g. “config/”).  
         – 0MODE is the permission string padded to four characters with a leading zero (e.g. 0600, 0755).  
       • Sort the lines alphabetically by NAME.  
       • Terminate the file with a single trailing newline character.  

    After writing the report, display its contents to the terminal so that a reviewer can immediately see the results.
truth:
Initial state before the agent starts:

    • Directory /home/user/audit_sample already exists and contains exactly the following four entries:

        1. /home/user/audit_sample/file1.conf
           – Type  : regular file  
           – Permissions: 0644  

        2. /home/user/audit_sample/script.sh
           – Type  : regular file  
           – Permissions: 0755  

        3. /home/user/audit_sample/secret.key
           – Type  : regular file  
           – Permissions: 0600  

        4. /home/user/audit_sample/config
           – Type  : directory  
           – Permissions: 0700  

    • The expected content of /home/user/permission_report.log **after** the agent finishes:

        config/: 0700
        file1.conf: 0644
        script.sh: 0755
        secret.key: 0600
        ⏎ (the final newline)

    The automated test will:

        1. Verify that /home/user/permission_report.log exists.  
        2. Verify that its contents are exactly as shown above (including the single trailing newline and alphabetical order).  
        3. Ensure the agent printed the same contents to STDOUT at least once during execution.
